content_en:
Each biographical entry in Tahdhib at-Tahdhib follows a consistent structure that provides the information scholars need to evaluate a narrator's reliability and understand their place in the transmission network. This standardized structure — developed from the earlier biographical tradition but refined by Ibn Hajar — became the model for subsequent biographical dictionaries in the hadith sciences field.

The entry begins with the narrator's full name chain (nasab) — personal name (ism), patronymic (kunyah), epithet (laqab), and tribal or regional attributions (nisbah). For common names, this information is essential for distinguishing between different narrators who share the same ism, and Ibn Hajar's entries are careful to provide enough identifying information that the specific narrator can be distinguished from all others with similar names.

Following the name comes information about the narrator's teachers — the scholars from whom they received hadith — and their students — the scholars who received hadith from them. This dual list of teachers and students locates the narrator in the transmission network: a scholar who knows who the narrator received from can verify whether they could plausibly have been in a position to receive from those teachers, and a scholar who knows who received from the narrator can identify which hadith collections are likely to contain their transmissions.

The core of each entry consists of the evaluations of the narrator by the major critics. Ibn Hajar compiled the assessments of Yahya ibn Ma'in, Ahmad ibn Hanbal, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Zur'ah, Abu Hatim ar-Razi, an-Nasa'i, Ibn Adi, and many others, quoting their specific language of evaluation wherever possible. This compilation of critical assessments allows the reader to see the range of opinion about the narrator and to weigh the agreement or disagreement among the critics.

The entry concludes with the year of the narrator's death (where known), their generation in the hadith transmission chain, and Ibn Hajar's own overall assessment of their reliability. This final assessment draws together the evidence from all the critics cited in the entry and represents Ibn Hajar's considered judgment. For the Taqrib at-Tahdhib, this final assessment was the only information retained, which is why the Taqrib, despite its brevity, provides sufficient guidance for most practical purposes of hadith criticism.
